# Why I stopped using a to-do app

Look, I tried them all. Things, OmniFocus, Todoist — you name it, I bought the annual plan. As it turns out, the trick wasn't picking the right app. The trick was admitting I wasn't going to read my own list.

Here's what happens. You spend a Saturday morning setting up contexts, perspectives, tags. Beautiful. Three weeks later you're declaring task bankruptcy and starting over with a new app. Give it a minute and you'll see the pattern.

So I switched to a single text file. `todo.md` in my home directory. No tags, no contexts, no perspectives. Three sections: today, this week, someday. That's it.

The thing is, you might possibly be tempted to think this is too simple. It's not. It works precisely because it's too simple to bankrupt. There's nothing to maintain. No system to debug. The cognitive overhead is essentially zero.

I open it in the morning. I look at "today". I do the things. End of day, anything I didn't do either moves to "this week" or gets deleted. No surprise there — most things deserve to be deleted.

The friction of moving an item from "this week" to "today" turns out to be exactly the right amount of friction. Apps make this too easy. You drag, you drop, suddenly today has 23 items and you're paralyzed again.
